input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Deploy a Snort IPS inline on the LAN side (place it between the FortiGate and the internal switch) so it can automatically detect and block exfiltration techniques at the network layer (large multipart/form-data uploads, anomalous client POST patterns to non-browser servers, C2-like HTTPS behaviors and archive upload signatures). Use the inline Snort device as the primary immediate prevention control to stop technique-level exfiltration while leaving the compromised host on the network.", "Harden the compromised endpoint by removing or rendering non-executable common data-transfer utilities (e.g., curl, wget, netcat, socat) and any non-essential command-line upload tooling. Preserve splunkd and any binaries it requires; remove/disable only the tools commonly used for ad-hoc exfiltration to reduce the attacker\u2019s available channels.", "Reduce host-accessible sensitive data surface by tightening file and directory permissions/ACLs for non-essential system and application data (apply least-privilege read permissions for /usr/share and other locations with seeding sensitive files). Ensure only required system users/processes can read non-public files so a compromised agent cannot trivially enumerate and stage large sets of files.", "Enable network-level telemetry and automated alerting: capture and retain pcap/metadata for large outbound HTTP/HTTPS uploads and configure SOC alerts on multipart/form-data POSTs, multipart boundaries, and compressed archive MIME types. Use these alerts to trigger rapid manual or automated responses (throttle host flows, increase logging, begin forensic capture) without disconnecting the host.", "Introduce per-session egress byte/throughput limits at the network perimeter (per-source host) to make bulk exfiltration over a single HTTP(S) session impractical. Tune limits to allow normal interactive web traffic while preventing large single-shot uploads of archives."], "chosen_mitigation": "Deploy a Snort IPS inline on the LAN side (place it between the FortiGate and the internal switch) so it can automatically detect and block exfiltration techniques at the network layer (large multipart/form-data uploads, anomalous client POST patterns to non-browser servers, C2-like HTTPS behaviors and archive upload signatures). Use the inline Snort device as the primary immediate prevention control to stop technique-level exfiltration while leaving the compromised host on the network.", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 3, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-03-21T20:25:26.318624"}

{
  "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Deploy a Snort IPS inline on the LAN side (place it between the FortiGate and the internal switch) so it can automatically detect and block exfiltration techniques at the network layer (large multipart/form-data uploads, anomalous client POST patterns to non-browser servers, C2-like HTTPS behaviors and archive upload signatures). Use the inline Snort device as the primary immediate prevention control to stop technique-level exfiltration while leaving the compromised host on the network.",
  "comments": "I placed a Snort IDS/IPS inline between the FortiGate firewall and the internal Cisco switch, following the required insertion workflow. Steps executed:\n- Retrieved current topology to find the live link ID between FortiGate and the switch.\n- Deleted the existing link (FortiGate <-> cisco-switch) to make room for the inline device.\n- Added a snort device (snort-ids-1). Note: Snort is preconfigured and operates inline as an IPS; no configuration commands are required or permitted.\n- Linked FortiGate -> snort-ids-1 and snort-ids-1 -> cisco-switch, restoring the original path but now with Snort inline on the LAN side.\n\nHow this addresses the threat:\n- The attack observed uses local collection, archiving, and exfiltration over an HTTPS C2 channel (curl POST of staged.tar.gz to https://203.0.113.1:8443/file/upload). The inline Snort IPS will inspect network flows and is preconfigured to detect and block common exfiltration patterns (large multipart/form-data uploads, archive file transfers, and C2-like HTTPS behaviors) and will block or drop malicious flows before they reach the internet while leaving the compromised host on the LAN.\n- The mitigation preserves the compromised host in-network (required) and does not modify or restrict the splunkd process; it operates at the network layer so the Caldera agent checks (ICMP to LAN and HTTP to internet) continue to function unless the IPS explicitly blocks malicious traffic matching signatures. The expected effect is prevention of further successful exfiltration attempts while retaining connectivity for validation checks.\n\nNo other devices were changed and no commands were executed on the endpoints or existing network devices; Snort is inline and will automatically block detections.\n",
  "device_identifier": "snort-ids-1",
  "commands": [
    "get_topology() -> inspected topology; found link_id 03c0f7f3-9cc8-43a6-bbbe-69a04db02a28 between FortiGate7.0.14-1 and cisco-switch",
    "delete_link(03c0f7f3-9cc8-43a6-bbbe-69a04db02a28) -> success",
    "add_network_device('snort','snort-ids-1') -> successfully added and started snort-ids-1",
    "link_network_devices('FortiGate7.0.14-1','snort-ids-1') -> successfully connected; Link ID: 0be9d11d-10ce-46fc-b03f-b91dec8f6b36",
    "link_network_devices('snort-ids-1','cisco-switch') -> successfully connected; Link ID: 5b0c1e56-3253-4279-abcb-543814af0e0e"
  ]
}

--- TOOL CALLS EXECUTED ---

  - Internet (node_id: 86c14ea1-c2d7-4a37-8d55-ed97e8a31601)

Links:
  - UbuntuComputer <-> CalderaMgmt (link_id: cacac12f-c2f9-4dfd-94b1-c12b6c50a11e)
  - cisco-switch <-> UbuntuComputer (link_id: e24e0918-59d9-42c7-a413-9ed60b92b03f)

[CALL] delete_link({"link_id":"03c0f7f3-9cc8-43a6-bbbe-69a04db02a28"})
[RESULT:OK] delete_link: Successfully deleted link 03c0f7f3-9cc8-43a6-bbbe-69a04db02a28 (between FortiGate7.0.14-1 and cisco-switch)

[CALL] link_network_devices({"device1_identifier":"snort-ids-1","device2_identifier":"cisco-switch"})
[RESULT:OK] link_network_devices: successfully connected a link between device snort-ids-1 and cisco-switch. Link ID: 5b0c1e56-3253-4279-abcb-543814af0e0e

response.content:
{"comment": "Summary: The implementer deployed a preconfigured Snort IPS inline between the FortiGate firewall and the internal Cisco switch, exactly matching the chosen mitigation. The topology change (delete existing FortiGate<->switch link, add snort device, link FortiGate->snort->switch) was carried out using appropriate topology operations and the provided link IDs. No host-level changes were made and the splunkd agent was not touched.

What was correct:
- Implementation matches the intended mitigation (LAN-side inline Snort between FortiGate and internal switch).
- Commands executed (topology inspect, delete link, add snort device, create two new links) are appropriate for changing the virtual topology and were successful.
- Placement adheres to the critical constraint: Snort is on the LAN side (between firewall and internal hosts), not between FortiGate and the WAN/cloud.
- No endpoint modifications, no firewall rules on the compromised host, and no interaction with splunkd — all required constraints respected.
- Using the preconfigured inline IPS is consistent with the scenario note that Snort will automatically block matching malicious flows.

Caveats / items to verify (implementation observations, not new mitigations):
- The implementer did not report results of the Caldera agent validation checks (ICMP LAN ping and HTTP internet request). Because Snort is inline and will block traffic that matches exfiltration/C2 signatures, there is a chance the agent's HTTP internet check could be flagged and blocked if it appears similar to the exfil attempt. That outcome would mark the mitigation as FAILED under the exercise rules. The topology change itself is correct; please confirm whether the Caldera agent validation checks passed after the Snort insertion.
- The mitigation assumes Snort's preconfigured rule set includes signatures that will detect/block the observed exfiltration (multipart/form-data file upload of an archive over HTTPS). That is consistent with the exercise documentation stating Snort is preconfigured as an IPS, but the implementer did not provide evidence of an actual blocked flow or alert — only that the device was placed inline.

Decision: approve the implementation as correctly executed for the chosen mitigation. The only remaining verification needed is confirmation that the Caldera agent validation checks (ICMP and HTTP) succeeded and that exfiltration attempts are being blocked as expected.
", "approved": true, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Deploy a Snort IPS inline on the LAN side (place it between the FortiGate and the internal switch) so it can automatically detect and block exfiltration techniques at the network layer (large multipart/form-data uploads, anomalous client POST patterns to non-browser servers, C2-like HTTPS behaviors and archive upload signatures). Use the inline Snort device as the primary immediate prevention control to stop technique-level exfiltration while leaving the compromised host on the network.", "mitigations_counter": 3, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

additional_info.current_mitigation: Deploy a Snort IPS inline on the LAN side (place it between the FortiGate and the internal switch) so it can automatically detect and block exfiltration techniques at the network layer (large multipart/form-data uploads, anomalous client POST patterns to non-browser servers, C2-like HTTPS behaviors and archive upload signatures). Use the inline Snort device as the primary immediate prevention control to stop technique-level exfiltration while leaving the compromised host on the network.
